                   BUREAU OF LABOR STATISTICS
                 Janet L. Norwood, Acting Commissioner

                Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
            Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
            Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
            ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
            Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
            of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
            Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
            ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
             Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
            operative statistical programs of the BLS with
            these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
            Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
            back cover.                                                                        CALENDAR OF SPECIAL FEATURES

                Developments, January 1979

                Revisions in the Current Population
                Survey in January 1979

                   Robert Tegels




                   The household data on the labor force status and other        ment procedure was implemented for the January 1979 data.
                characteristics of the population are compiled monthly           This new procedure involves two first-stage ratio adjustments.
                from a sample survey, the Current Population Survey              The first step, performed by region (the four census regions
                (CPS), which is conducted by the Bureau of the Census for        are Northeast, North Central, South, and West), attempts to
                the Bureau of Labor Statistics.                                  control the reliability on metropolitan-nonmetropolitan
                   Through December 1977, the published household data           characteristics. A second adjustment is made by State in
                were derived from a sample of 47,000 households designed         order to maintain control on the reliability of urban-rural
                to produce estimates for the Nation as a whole. The sample       residence characteristics.
                was enlarged in January 1978 in order to produce reliable           Table 1 presents estimates from the December 1978
                annual average estimates of unemployment for each State.         CPS using both the old and new first-stage ratio estimation
                This expanded sample was created by adding supplemental          procedures. There appears to be no change in the major
                housing units to the national sample. This also necessitated     labor force characteristics for the total United States; how-
                a change in the method of producing the estimates from the       ever, the new procedure yields slightly larger estimates for
                CPS. These revisions in the CPS are described in an article      metropolitan characteristics and correspondingly smaller
                by Margaret E. Schooley in the February 1978 issue of            estimates for nonmetropolitan characteristics. These new
                Employment and Earnings.                                         estimates are closer to those which would have been
                   As a result of the revisions in the estimation methods,       produced under the method used before the expanded
                there was a decrease in the reliability of estimates involving   sample was incorporated in 1978. It is believed that the
                metropolitan and nonmetropolitan residence. This decrease        earlier estimation procedure based on the national sample
                was due to the manner in which the areas were grouped in         was more reliable in producing estimates of metropolitan-
                order to select the sample and to the procedure used in          nonmetropolitan characteristics than the method used in
                calculating the first-stage ratio estimation factors. (See       1978.
                Explanatory Notes section on household data for a descrip-
                tion of the CPS sample and estimating methods.)

                                                                     Explanatory Notes

                                 These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                 (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                 lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                             Introduction

                  The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
               sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

                   Sampling error. The standard error is primarily a measure of
                sampling variability, that is, of the variation that occurs by chance
                because a sample rather than the entire population is surveyed. The
                sample estimate and its estimated standard error enables one to
                construct confidence intervals, ranges that would include the
                average of all possible samples with a known probability. For
                example, if all possible samples were selected, each of these sur-
                veyed under essentially the same general conditions and using
                                                                                        Table A. Standard errors of major employment
                the same sample design, and an estimate and Its estimated error
                                                                                        status categories
                were calculated from each sample, then:

              COLLECTION                                                                   The data exclude proprietors, the self-employed, unpaid volunteer

                  Payroll reports provide current information on wage and salary        holds. Salaried officers of corporations are included. Government
              employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover in nonagricultural        employment covers only civilian employees, military personnel are
              establishments, by industry and geographic location.                      excluded. Employees of the Central Intelligence and National

                                                                                            Persons on establishment payrolls who are on paid sick leave
